flashcards.back: A fully managed Platform as a Service (PaaS) from Google that allows developers to build and deploy web applications without managing servers, VMs, or OS patches. GAE handles provisioning, scaling, and load balancing automatically.

flashcards.front: What are the two GAE runtime environments?
flashcards.back: Standard Environment (sandboxed, fast startup, scales to zero, more restrictive) and Flexible Environment (Docker containers on GCE VMs, more customization, any language/library, SSH access).

flashcards.front: Name four key built-in services provided by GAE.
flashcards.back: Datastore (NoSQL database), Memcache (in-memory caching), Task Queues (async task execution), and URL Fetch (outbound HTTP requests).

flashcards.front: How does GAE (PaaS) differ from AWS EC2 (IaaS)?
flashcards.back: GAE: developers manage only application code, no server access, automatic scaling. EC2: full OS/server control, manual or configured scaling, you manage OS patches and middleware.

flashcards.front: What are the equivalent PaaS services across GAE, AWS, and Azure?
flashcards.back: Google App Engine (GAE), AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and Azure App Service are all PaaS platforms for deploying web applications without managing infrastructure.

flashcards.front: What is a unique scaling feature of GAE?
flashcards.back: GAE can automatically scale down to zero instances when there is no traffic, reducing costs to near zero during idle periods.

flashcards.front: What configuration file is used to define a GAE application?
flashcards.back: app.yaml (for Python/Go/Node.js) or appengine-web.xml (for Java). It defines runtime, URL routing, scaling parameters, and service configurations.
